modules.title: Module 1: Python Basics & Variables
modules.description: Learn print statements, comments, variables, and basic data types.

steps.title: 1. Making Python Speak
steps.concept:
In Python, the print() function is used to display output to the screen. It is one of the most fundamental tools in programming. Here is how it functions:

  • Syntax: Place whatever you want to display inside parentheses ( ). Text must be enclosed in quotes (either double " or single ').
  • Multiple Items: You can print multiple things at once by separating them with commas (e.g., print("Hello", "World")), which Python will automatically separate with spaces.
steps.instructions: Click Load Demo Code to load this example into your editor, then click Run Code to run it!

steps.title: 2. Writing Notes (Comments)
steps.concept:
Comments are notes written in plain English to explain what the code is doing. Python completely ignores comments when executing programs. Here is how they work:

  • Single-line Comments: Start the line with a hash symbol #. Anything on that line after the # is treated as a comment.
  • Inline Comments: You can write code first, followed by a # on the same line to annotate that specific line of code.
  • Purpose: Used to temporarily disable blocks of code for testing, document complicated logic, or leave notes for other developers.
steps.demo_code:
# This is a comment. Python ignores this!
print("Python is running this line!")
steps.instructions: Click Load Demo Code and click Run Code. You will see that only the text inside the print function is printed!

steps.title: 3. Variables (Labeled Drawers)
steps.concept:
A variable acts as a storage box or labeled drawer in the computer's memory. You store data in it, give it a name, and access it later using that name. Here is how it functions:

  • Assignment Operator (=): In programming, a single equals sign does NOT mean "equal to"—it assigns the value on the right to the variable name on the left.
  • Naming Rules: Must start with a letter or underscore, can only contain letters, numbers, and underscores, and cannot contain spaces (use snake_case, e.g., favorite_animal).
  • Reassignment: You can change the value of a variable at any time; Python will overwrite the old value with the new one.
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change "Panda" to your favorite animal's name, then click Run Code.

steps.title: 4. Numbers and Decimals (Integers & Floats)
steps.concept:
Python classifies numbers into distinct types because computers handle them differently under the hood:

  • Integers (int): Whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) with no fractional part, e.g., 10 or -5.
  • Floats (float): Numbers with a decimal point, e.g., 1.99 or 0.0.
  • Math Operations: Python natively supports arithmetic operators like addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/). Dividing two integers always returns a float!
steps.demo_code:
candies = 10
price = 1.99
total_cost = candies * price
print(total_cost)
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change candies to 5 and click Run Code to calculate the new total cost!

steps.title: 5. Text Formatting (F-Strings)
steps.concept:
An F-string (formatted string literal) is a powerful way to inject variables directly inside text without messy concatenation. Here is how it functions:

  • Structure: Put the letter f or F immediately before the opening quotes of your string.
  • Variables inside braces: Write variable names or expressions directly inside curly braces { }. Python will automatically evaluate and format them as text.
  • Cleanliness: It keeps your code extremely readable compared to using string addition (+).
steps.demo_code:
name = "Bob"
score = 95
print(f"{name} scored {score}% on the quiz!")
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the name from "Bob" to your own name and run the code!

labs.title: Exercise 1: Labeled Boxes
labs.instructions:
Create a variable named student_name and set it to "Alex". Then, create a variable named hours_studied and set it to the number 4.

Concept Tip: Variable names are case-sensitive. Do not use quotes around integers (like 4), or Python will treat them as text.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
student_name = ""
hours_studied = 0
labs.example_code:
# Example of creating variables:
player_name = "Mario"
score = 150
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
student_name = "Alex"
hours_studied = 4
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a variable named employee_name and set it to "Sarah". Then, create a variable named years_service and set it to the number 5.

Concept Tip: Place string values in quotes, but store numbers without quotes so you can perform math operations on them later.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
employee_name = ""
years_service = 0
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of creating variables:
player_name = "Luigi"
score = 250
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
employee_name = "Sarah"
years_service = 5

labs.title: Exercise 2: Basic Calculations
labs.instructions:
Create a variable base_salary and set it to 1200. Create a variable bonus and set it to 350. Finally, create a variable total_pay and set it to the sum of the two variables.

Concept Tip: Instead of writing total_pay = 1550, write total_pay = base_salary + bonus. This references the values dynamically, which is standard practice in software development.
labs.example_code:
# Example of performing basic math on variables:
item_price = 10
tax = 2
total_price = item_price + tax
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
base_salary = 1200
bonus = 350
total_pay = base_salary + bonus
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a variable rent and set it to 800. Create a variable utilities and set it to 150. Finally, create a variable total_expenses and set it to the sum of the two variables.

Concept Tip: Reference the variable names in your sum expression so changes to rent or utilities automatically update total_expenses.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of adding variables:
item_price = 100
shipping = 15
total_cost = item_price + shipping
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
rent = 800
utilities = 150
total_expenses = rent + utilities

labs.title: Exercise 3: String Formatting (F-Strings)
labs.instructions:
Create a variable city with value "Paris" and country with value "France". Then create travel_message using an F-string that evaluates to: "I want to visit Paris, France."

Concept Tip: The F-string must start with f" and inject variables like this: f"I want to visit {city}, {country}.". Pay close attention to the comma and spacing.
labs.example_code:
# Example of using F-strings to format text:
fruit = "apple"
color = "red"
message = f"The {fruit} is {color}."
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
city = "Paris"
country = "France"
travel_message = f"I want to visit {city}, {country}."
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a variable movie with value "Toy Story" and genre with value "Animation". Then create movie_message using an F-string that evaluates to: "The movie Toy Story is in the Animation genre."

Concept Tip: Prefix the string with f and wrap variables movie and genre in curly braces inside the string.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of using F-strings:
pet = "dog"
name = "Buddy"
status_msg = f"My pet {pet} is named {name}."
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
movie = "Toy Story"
genre = "Animation"
movie_message = f"The movie {movie} is in the {genre} genre."

labs.title: Exercise 4: Slices (Modulo & Division)
labs.instructions:
Create a variable total_slices set to 15. Create slices_per_person and set it to the number of whole slices each of 4 people get (using floor division //). Create leftover_slices and set it to the remainder (using modulo %).

Concept Tip: Floor division (//) rounds division down to the nearest whole integer. Modulo (%) returns only the remainder. Example: 15 // 4 is 3, and 15 % 4 is 3.
labs.example_code:
# Example of whole division (floor division) and remainder (modulo):
total_cookies = 10
cookies_per_child = total_cookies // 3
remaining_cookies = total_cookies % 3
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
total_slices = 15
slices_per_person = total_slices // 4
leftover_slices = total_slices % 4
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a variable total_candies set to 22. Create candies_per_child and set it to the number of whole candies each of 5 children get (using floor division //). Create leftover_candies and set it to the remainder (using modulo %).

Concept Tip: 22 // 5 will give you the count of full candies per child (integer), and 22 % 5 will yield the remaining candies that cannot be evenly shared.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of division and modulo:
total_pencils = 17
pencils_per_box = total_pencils // 6
remaining_pencils = total_pencils % 6
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
total_candies = 22
candies_per_child = total_candies // 5
leftover_candies = total_candies % 5

modules.title: Module 2: Control Flow & Logic
modules.description: Learn comparisons, conditional statements, logical operators, and loops.

steps.title: 1. Comparing Values
steps.concept:
Comparison operators compare two values and evaluate to a Boolean: True (yes) or False (no). Here is how they function:

  • Operators: Use == for equality, != for inequality, > for greater than, < for less than, >= for greater-or-equal, and <= for less-or-equal.
  • Equality warning: Double equals (==) is for comparison, whereas single equals (=) is for assigning variables. Mixing them up causes code errors.
steps.demo_code:
cookies = 5
print("Is cookies > 3?", cookies > 3)
print("Is cookies equal to 10?", cookies == 10)
steps.instructions: Load the demo code and run it to see how Python evaluates comparison expressions.

steps.title: 2. If, Elif, and Else Decisions
steps.concept:
Conditional statements let your code make decisions and execute different paths based on conditions. Here is how they function:

  • The if: The starting point. It evaluates a condition; if True, it runs the block below.
  • The elif: Short for "else if". Used to check multiple conditions sequentially if the previous ones were False.
  • The else: The fallback block. Runs only if all preceding conditions evaluate to False.
  • Indentation: Python requires 4 spaces of indentation for lines inside code blocks. Failing to indent causes immediate syntax errors!
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the temperature variable to 5 and run it to see Python switch to the coat path.

steps.title: 3. Repeating Tasks (For Loops)
steps.concept:
A for loop repeats a block of code a set number of times. Here is how it functions:

  • The range(start, end): Generates a sequence of integers starting at start and going up to, but not including, end. For example, range(1, 5) generates 1, 2, 3, 4.
  • Loop Variable: In for x in range(...):, the variable x is assigned the current value of the range in each turn automatically.
steps.demo_code:
for count in range(1, 4):
 print(f"This is loop turn {count}")
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the range to count from 1 to 6 and run the code!

steps.title: 4. Loops with Conditions (While Loops)
steps.concept:
A while loop runs continuously as long as a condition is True. Here is how it functions:

  • Condition Check: The loop checks the condition before every turn. If True, the code inside is executed. If False, the loop stops.
  • Infinite Loop Hazard: If the condition never becomes False, the loop runs forever, freezing the program. Always update variables inside the loop (e.g., decrementing a counter) to guarantee the loop will terminate!
steps.demo_code:
battery = 3
while battery > 0:
 print(f"Phone active. Battery: {battery}%")
 battery = battery - 1
print("Battery dead!")
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Notice how the battery level decreases by 1 in each turn until it stops.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Number Sign Classifier
labs.instructions:
Create a variable number set to -7.5. Create a variable classification and use an if-elif-else statement to set it to "positive", "negative", or "zero" depending on the value of number.

Concept Tip: Check if number > 0 first, then use elif number < 0, and fall back to else for zero. Indent the lines inside each block.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
number = -7.5
classification = ""
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a variable score set to 85. Create a variable result_status and use an if-elif-else statement to set it to "pass" (if score >= 60), "fail" (if score < 60), or "error" (if score is less than 0 or greater than 100).

Concept Tip: Handle the boundary cases carefully. Check error cases first (e.g., score < 0 or score > 100) to filter invalid scores before checking passing or failing marks.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
score = 85
result_status = ""

labs.title: Exercise 2: Multiply by 10 (Loops)
labs.instructions:
Create an empty list called results. Use a for loop to count from 1 to 10. Inside, multiply the current number by 10 and add (.append()) the result to results.

Concept Tip: The loop should run over range(1, 11) to count from 1 to 10 inclusive. Use results.append(value) to build up the list.
labs.example_code:
# Example of creating a list and adding items using a loop:
values = []
for x in range(1, 5):
 values.append(x * 2)
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
results = []
for i in range(1, 11):
 results.append(i * 10)
labs.instructions_variant:
Create an empty list called squares. Use a for loop to count from 1 to 5. Inside, square the current number (multiply it by itself, or use ** 2) and add (.append()) the result to squares.

Concept Tip: Set the range of your loop from 1 to 6. Multiply the loop variable by itself to calculate the square and append it to your list.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of building a list with loops:
doubles = []
for x in range(1, 6):
 doubles.append(x * 2)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
squares = []
for i in range(1, 6):
 squares.append(i * i)

labs.title: Exercise 3: Battery Charge (While Loops)
labs.instructions:
Create variables charge = 0 and ticks = 0. Write a while loop that runs while charge < 100. Inside the loop, increase charge by 15 and ticks by 1.

Concept Tip: The condition is charge < 100. Make sure both additions are inside the loop block and indented by 4 spaces so they update every iteration.
labs.initial_code:
# Write your code below:
charge = 0
ticks = 0
labs.example_code:
# Example of running a loop while a condition is true:
water_level = 0
steps = 0
while water_level < 50:
 water_level = water_level + 10
 steps = steps + 1
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
charge = 0
ticks = 0
while charge < 100:
 charge = charge + 15
 ticks = ticks + 1
labs.instructions_variant:
Create variables distance = 0 and steps = 0. Write a while loop that runs while distance < 80. Inside the loop, increase distance by 12 and steps by 1.

Concept Tip: Set up the loop header as while distance < 80:. If you forget to update distance inside the loop, the condition will remain True forever, causing an infinite loop.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Write your code below:
distance = 0
steps = 0
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of while loop count:
level = 0
iterations = 0
while level < 50:
 level = level + 8
 iterations = iterations + 1
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
distance = 0
steps = 0
while distance < 80:
 distance = distance + 12
 steps = steps + 1

modules.title: Module 3: Data Structures
modules.description: Learn Lists, Dictionaries, Tuples, and Sets.

steps.title: 1. Lists (Ordered Collections)
steps.concept:
A list is an ordered, changeable (mutable) collection of items written inside square brackets [ ]. Lists allow us to store multiple values in a single variable. Here is how they work:

  • Zero-Based Indexing: Python counts positions starting from 0. The first item is at index 0, the second at 1, and so on.
  • Negative Indexing: You can count backward from the end of the list. Index -1 is the last item, -2 is the second to last, etc.
  • Heterogeneous Items: A single list can contain different types of data (strings, integers, floats, and even other lists!).
steps.demo_code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print("First fruit:", fruits[0])
print("Last fruit:", fruits[-1])
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Try changing index 0 to index 1 to get the banana!

steps.title: 2. Modifying Lists
steps.concept:
Lists are mutable, meaning they can be modified after creation. Here are the core methods for altering a list:

  • .append(item): Adds the specified item to the very end of the list.
  • .remove(item): Searches for the first occurrence of the specified item in the list and removes it. If the item doesn't exist, it raises an error.
  • len(list_name): A built-in function that returns the total count of elements inside the list.
steps.demo_code:
playlist = ["Song A", "Song B"]
playlist.append("Song C")
playlist.remove("Song A")
print(playlist)
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it to see the updated playlist.

steps.title: 3. Dictionaries (Key-Value Lookups)
steps.concept:
A dictionary (often called a dict) is an unordered, mutable collection of key-value pairs written inside curly braces { }. It functions like a real-world dictionary or phone directory: you look up a unique word (the key) to find its definition (the value).

  • Keys and Values: Each pair is structured as key: value. Keys must be unique and immutable (such as strings or numbers), while values can be anything.
  • Fast Lookups: Retrieving a value using its key (e.g., my_dict["key"]) is extremely fast under the hood because of a technique called hashing.
  • Adding/Updating: Assigning a value to a key (e.g., my_dict["new_key"] = value) will add the pair if the key is new, or overwrite the existing value if the key already exists.
steps.demo_code:
prices = {"apple": 0.99, "banana": 0.59}
prices["orange"] = 0.79 # Adds orange
print("An apple costs:", prices["apple"]) 
print(prices)
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the apple price to 1.20 and run the code.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Shopping List Manager
labs.instructions:
Create a list shopping_list containing "apples", "milk", and "bread". Add "eggs" to the end. Remove "milk". Find the length and store it in list_length.

Concept Tip: Initialize the list with square brackets. Call .append("eggs") and .remove("milk"). Find the list size using the built-in len(shopping_list) function.
labs.example_code:
# Example of list operations:
colors = ["red", "blue"]
colors.append("green") # Adds to the end
colors.remove("red") # Removes by value
num_colors = len(colors) # Finds length
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
shopping_list = ["apples", "milk", "bread"]
shopping_list.append("eggs")
shopping_list.remove("milk")
list_length = len(shopping_list)
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a list todo_list containing "clean", "study", and "exercise". Add "read" to the end. Remove "study". Find the length and store it in todo_length.

Concept Tip: Call todo_list.append("read"), followed by todo_list.remove("study"), and measure the list with len(todo_list).
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of list append and remove:
tags = ["a", "b"]
tags.append("c")
tags.remove("b")
tags_count = len(tags)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
todo_list = ["clean", "study", "exercise"]
todo_list.append("read")
todo_list.remove("study")
todo_length = len(todo_list)

labs.title: Exercise 2: Inventory Lookup
labs.instructions:
Create a dictionary inventory with keys: "apples" set to 12, "oranges" set to 5, and "bananas" set to 15. Retrieve the bananas quantity and store it in bananas_qty.

Concept Tip: Write key-value pairs separated by colons inside curly braces: {"apples": 12, ...}. Retrieve values using brackets: inventory["bananas"].
labs.instructions_variant:
Create a dictionary prices with keys: "milk" set to 3.50, "bread" set to 2.20, and "cheese" set to 4.80. Retrieve the cheese price and store it in cheese_cost.

Concept Tip: Initialize keys and values, and extract cheese cost using the key name: prices["cheese"].

labs.title: Exercise 3: Common Colors (Sets)
labs.instructions:
Alice likes colors ["blue", "green", "red", "green"]. Bob likes ["green", "yellow", "blue"]. Convert both lists to sets (alice_set and bob_set), and find their common colors as a set in shared_colors using the intersection .intersection() method.

Concept Tip: A set is a collection of unique elements. Converting a list to a set automatically filters out duplicates. Find common values using set_a.intersection(set_b).
labs.example_code:
# Example of converting lists to sets and finding common items:
team_a = set(["apple", "banana"])
team_b = set(["banana", "cherry"])
common_items = team_a.intersection(team_b)
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
alice_set = set(["blue", "green", "red", "green"])
bob_set = set(["green", "yellow", "blue"])
shared_colors = alice_set.intersection(bob_set)
labs.instructions_variant:
Group A has members ["cat", "dog", "bird", "dog"]. Group B has members ["dog", "fish", "cat"]. Convert both lists to sets (set_a and set_b), and find their common members in shared_members using the intersection .intersection() method.

Concept Tip: Wrap the list in a set() call (e.g. set(list_variable)). Use the intersection method to calculate the overlap between the two sets.
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example of finding set intersection:
s1 = set([1, 2, 3])
s2 = set([2, 3, 4])
common = s1.intersection(s2)
labs.help_text_variant:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
set_a = set(["cat", "dog", "bird", "dog"])
set_b = set(["dog", "fish", "cat"])
shared_members = set_a.intersection(set_b)

modules.title: Module 4: Functions & Reusable Code
modules.description: Learn parameters, return statements, scope, and libraries.

steps.title: 1. Writing Custom Functions
steps.concept:
A function is a named, reusable block of code designed to perform a specific task. Here is how they function:

  • Definition (def): Start with the keyword def, followed by the function name, parentheses containing optional input variables (called parameters), and a colon.
  • Execution (Return): Use the return statement to send the calculated result back to the caller. Code execution exits the function immediately when it encounters a return.
  • Modularity: Functions stop you from writing repetitive code and make programs easier to debug.
steps.demo_code:
def double_value(number):
 return number * 2

print(double_value(5))
print(double_value(12))
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Call the function with double_value(50) and print the output.

steps.title: 2. Default Values for Parameters
steps.concept:
Python functions allow you to specify default values for parameters. Here is how they function:

  • Fallback value: If the caller provides an argument, Python uses the caller's value. If they omit it, Python falls back to the default value.
  • Syntax: Set the default value inside the function header, e.g., def greet(name="Friend"):.
  • Ordering: Parameters with default values MUST be placed after parameters without default values (positional arguments first).
steps.demo_code:
def greet(name="Friend"):
 return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))
print(greet()) # Uses the default 'Friend'
steps.instructions: Load the demo. Change the default name "Friend" to "Explorer" and run it.

steps.title: 3. Python Modules (Toolboxes)
steps.concept:
A module is a file containing pre-written Python code, definitions, and functions that you can include in your own scripts. Here is how they function:

  • The import Statement: Load an external module using import module_name.
  • Dot Notation: Call functions inside that module using the syntax module_name.function_name().
  • Examples: The math module provides mathematical utilities (e.g. math.sqrt(64)), and the random module offers random number generation (e.g. random.randint(1, 6)).
steps.demo_code:
import math
import random

print("Square root of 64 is:", math.sqrt(64))
print("Dice roll result:", random.randint(1, 6))
steps.instructions: Load the demo and run it. Click run multiple times to see different random dice rolls!

labs.title: Exercise 1: Fahrenheit to Celsius
labs.instructions:
Write a function to_celsius(fahrenheit) that converts temperature using the formula: (fahrenheit - 32) * 5 / 9 and returns the Celsius equivalent.

Concept Tip: Define the function with def to_celsius(fahrenheit):. Calculate the result and send it back using the return keyword.
labs.instructions_variant:
Write a function inches_to_cm(inches) that converts length using the formula: inches * 2.54 and returns the centimeter equivalent.

Concept Tip: Define your function with a parameter named inches, and return the conversion math directly.

labs.title: Exercise 2: Power Calculator
labs.instructions:
Write a function power(base, exponent=2) that returns base raised to the power of exponent.

Concept Tip: Set the default parameter inside the function header: def power(base, exponent=2):. In Python, raise a number to a power using the exponentiation operator **.
labs.instructions_variant:
Write a function multiply(a, b=5) that returns a multiplied by b.

Concept Tip: Add the parameter b with a default value of 5. In the body of the function, return the product of a and b.

labs.title: Exercise 3: Roller Coaster Entry Check
labs.instructions:
Write a function can_ride(height, age) that returns True if height >= 48 AND age >= 8. Otherwise, return False.

Concept Tip: Use the logical operator and to combine both checks. Your function can return the result of the comparison directly: return height >= 48 and age >= 8.
labs.instructions_variant:
Write a function can_vote(is_registered, age) that returns True if is_registered is True AND age >= 18. Otherwise, return False.

Concept Tip: Combine the registered state and age checks using the and operator. Return True if both conditions evaluate to True, and False otherwise.

modules.title: Module 5: Interactive Guessing Game
modules.description: Combine your knowledge to build an interactive number guessing game.

steps.title: 1. Project Brief: Guessing Game
steps.concept:
You are going to build a full, interactive High-Low Guessing Game! This project acts as a capstone that combines the core programming blocks you've learned. Here is how they function together:

  • Random Secret Number: Generated using the random.randint(1, 50) function.
  • User Input Loop: A while attempts_left > 0: loop collects guesses and manages user attempts.
  • Input Parsing: The string returned by input() is converted to an integer using int() so you can perform numeric comparisons.
  • Conditional Feedback: Checks if the guess is too high, too low, or correct, and terminates the loop when the player wins.
steps.demo_code:
# Ready to start building your game in the Lab tab?
print("Click on the Lab tab above to start coding!")
steps.instructions: Read the blueprint explanation on the left, then click on the Lab tab above to build the project.

labs.title: Exercise 1: Build the Guessing Game
labs.instructions:
Write a full High-Low Guessing Game! The computer chooses a random number from 1 to 50. The player gets 6 attempts. Use input() to read guesses, convert them to integers, decrement attempts, and tell the player if they are too high, too low, or won! (Check the solution tab if you get stuck).

Concept Tip: Loop while attempts_left > 0. Prompt the player for input, cast it with int(), decrement attempts, and output: "Too low!", "Too high!", or "You won!". If they guess correctly, use the break statement to exit the loop immediately.
labs.initial_code:
# High-Low Guessing Game
import random

secret_number = random.randint(1, 50)
attempts_left = 6

print("I am thinking of a number between 1 and 50!")
# Start your loop here...
labs.example_code:
# Example structure of an interactive console loop:
import random
secret = random.randint(1, 10)

# Read input and convert to integer
user_input = input("Guess a number from 1 to 10: ")
guess = int(user_input)

if guess == secret:
 print("Correct!")
else:
 print(f"Wrong, it was {secret}")
labs.help_text:
Try writing exactly this in the editor:
import random
secret_number = random.randint(1, 50)
attempts_left = 6
print("I am thinking of a number between 1 and 50!")
while attempts_left > 0:
    guess = int(input("Take a guess: "))
    attempts_left = attempts_left - 1
    if guess == secret_number:
        print("You won!")
        break
    elif guess < secret_number:
        print("Too low!")
    else:
        print("Too high!")
if attempts_left == 0 and guess != secret_number:
    print(f"Game over. The number was {secret_number}.")
labs.instructions_variant:
Write a custom number guessing game where the computer picks a random secret number from 1 to 20, and the player gets 4 attempts to guess it. Output "Too low!", "Too high!", or "You won!".

Concept Tip: Initialize the secret number with random.randint(1, 20) and attempts to 4. Use conditional branches inside a while loop to check the player's guess against the secret number, and decrement the attempts count after every turn.
labs.initial_code_variant:
# Variant Guessing Game (1 to 20, 4 attempts)
import random

secret_number = random.randint(1, 20)
attempts_left = 4

print("Guess a number from 1 to 20!")
# Start your loop here...
labs.example_code_variant:
# Example structure of an interactive console loop:
import random
secret = random.randint(1, 5)
guess = int(input("Guess (1-5): "))
if guess == secret:
 print("Got it!")
else:
 print("Miss!")

import random
secret_number = random.randint(1, 20)
attempts_left = 4
print("Guess a number from 1 to 20!")
while attempts_left > 0:
    guess = int(input("Take a guess: "))
    attempts_left = attempts_left - 1
    if guess == secret_number:
        print("You won!")
        break
    elif guess < secret_number:
        print("Too low!")
    else:
        print("Too high!")
